column2: It focuses on intellectual training rather than just practical skills.
Society depends on the specific skills provided by professionals.
The profession holds a monopoly over who can provide the service through accreditation and licensing systems.
Professionals rely on their own individual judgement.
The profession uses codes of conduct and standards of practice to regulate itself.
A profession has a specific internal aim or good that constitutes the practice. This aim is morally justifiable.
It is a cooperative human activity aimed at achieving specific standards of excellence appropriate to that practice.
Professions are seen as practices with an inherently moral character that aim at a moral goal.
A profession consists of individuals in the same occupation who are voluntarily organized.
Professionals earn a living by openly serving a moral ideal.
Professionals serve their moral ideal in ways that go beyond what the law, morality, and public opinion require.
The primary motivation in the business model is economic self-interest and making profits.
Specialized knowledge is treated merely as a commodity to gain a monopoly and increase profit.
The law is the major constraint. Claims of high ethical self-regulation are just a smokescreen to keep authorities away and gain social status.
A trust relationship and agreement exists between the profession and society.
The profession has a duty to attain and retain specialized knowledge to serve the public good.
If the profession fulfills its duties, society grants it privileges like autonomy and internal self-regulation.
The 'base' moral beliefs that are supported by almost everyone.
Generally shared societal ideas about what is right and wrong.
The moral beliefs that a person subscribes to personally.
Situations where personal ethics clash with professional ethics, leading to objections based on conscience.
The specific ethical standards upheld by a professional community.
Rules that are usually formulated in an explicit, formal code of a society or institute.
Professional ethics usually takes priority over a person's individual personal ethics.
The negative face of professional ethics is based on the principle of 'First, do no harm'.
It focuses on preventing harm and malpractice through rules and prohibitions, often inspired by past disasters.
The positive face of ethics focused on the promotion of public interest and well-being.
Based on the principle to 'Do good' and focusing on the positive role technology plays to improve humankind.
Acting with fidelity, honesty, confidentiality, and disclosing conflicts of interest.
Maintaining the required education, training, and experience for the profession.
Prioritizing the health, safety, and well-being of the public.
Avoiding adverse impacts on the environment so future generations are not compromised.
Upholding the standing and reputation of the engineering profession.
Ethical decision-making is not an algorithm but requires the meaningful interpretation of general principles using judgement.
Engineering decisions must be made in practical real life with time, financial, and other resource constraints.
Engineering practice involves problem-solving that is riddled with complexities and uncertainties.
Because perfect decisions are often impossible, professionals rely on certain habits, virtues, and character traits to cope and act ethically.
